# Agentic Coding Interfaces in Clinical Research

## From Passive Tools to Active Agents

The trajectory of LLM deployment in clinical research has undergone a fundamental paradigm shift: from isolated single-turn prompts, to chained workflows, and ultimately to fully agentic systems capable of autonomous planning, tool invocation, memory management, and multi-step reasoning across heterogeneous data sources. An agentic AI system in healthcare can be defined as an LLM-based architecture that perceives its environment, selects from a repertoire of tools — including web browsers, code interpreters, database APIs, and retrieval systems — executes multi-step plans, and iterates based on intermediate outputs, all without requiring human instruction at each step. Where a chat-based system responds to a single prompt in isolation, an agentic system decomposes a high-level research goal into sub-tasks, calls appropriate tools in sequence, verifies intermediate results, and synthesizes a final output that no single prompt could produce. The architectural requirements for such systems — a planner, an executor, and a verifier component — are precisely the design evaluated by Liu and colleagues in their benchmark study of clinical agent systems [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. This shift fundamentally changes the labor economics of clinical investigation, opening computational research pipelines to investigators who lack traditional programming expertise.

## Multi-Agent Systems in Clinical Research

Benchmarking work has begun to quantify both the promise and the persistent limitations of agentic architectures on clinical tasks. Liu and colleagues evaluated two systems — the open-source OpenManus built on Meta's Llama-4 and the proprietary Manus platform employing a multistep planner-executor-verifier architecture — across AgentClinic, MedAgentsBench, and Humanity's Last Exam [@Liu2026Benchmarking]. Despite access to web browsing, code execution, and file editing, agent systems yielded only modest accuracy gains over baseline LLMs: 60.3% on AgentClinic MedQA, 30.3% on MedAgentsBench, and 15.5% on multimodal tasks. These gains came at substantial cost — more than tenfold token usage and more than twofold latency — and although 89.9% of hallucinations were filtered by in-agent safeguards, hallucinations remained prevalent, exposing a central tension: agentic architecture amplifies the reach of LLMs without proportionally amplifying their accuracy.

The broader benchmarking literature deepens this picture. Gong and colleagues synthesized 39 medical LLM benchmarks and documented a striking knowledge-practice gap: leading models achieve 84–90% accuracy on USMLE-style knowledge examinations yet fall to DiagnosisArena 45.82% (95% CI 42.9–48.8%), MedAgentBench 69.67% (95% CI 64.2–74.6%), and HealthBench 60% (95% CI 58.6–61.3%) on practice-oriented tasks, with safety assessment reaching only 40–50% accuracy [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. This knowledge-practice disparity is magnified in agentic pipelines, where errors at upstream steps compound downstream. The MedHELM framework, which evaluated nine frontier LLMs across a clinician-validated taxonomy of 121 specific clinical tasks, found that advanced reasoning models achieved win rates of 66%, though Claude 3.5 Sonnet delivered comparable results at 15% lower computational cost [@Bedi2026Holistic]. Crucially, MedHELM's taxonomy includes a medical research category spanning literature analysis and clinical data analysis — precisely the tasks that agentic coding interfaces are designed to automate.

## Coding Interfaces and Automation

The capacity for LLMs to write, execute, and iteratively refine code against clinical databases represents the most structurally novel expression of the agentic paradigm. Wang and colleagues deployed a GPT-4o-based pipeline to validate 13,102 ICD-10-CM codes drawn from 865,079 assigned codes in the MIMIC-IV database, achieving 93.6% accuracy, 95.4% sensitivity, and 85.2% specificity — comparable to physician review and offering a scalable auditing mechanism for administrative and research data [@Wang2026Validation]. The neuro-symbolic architecture developed by Prenosil and colleagues represents an equally rigorous instantiation: GPT-4 extracted candidate facts from 206 consecutive prostate cancer PET/CT reports — generating 5,356 data points covering 26 parameters per report — while a rule-based expert system verified each fact against formal medical rules [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic]. The hybrid system achieved F1 scores of 1.00 for both study inclusion and recurrence detection with 100% PSA accuracy, compared to GPT-4 alone at 0.63 and 0.95 respectively, while simultaneously providing an auditable reasoning chain and intercepting reports with residual patient identifiers.

LLM-driven agentic pipelines extend naturally to medication-level EHR extraction. Shao and colleagues demonstrated scalable medication extraction and discontinuation identification from electronic health records, establishing that tasks requiring nuanced temporal reasoning can be delegated to automated agents for research purposes [@Shao2026Scalable]. Knowledge graph integration further advances the paradigm: the GraphRAG-enabled local LLM developed by Evangelista and colleagues for gestational diabetes management — built on 1,212 peer-reviewed articles — achieved a BERTScore of 0.98, outperforming ChatGPT, Claude, and BioMistral comparators [@Evangelista2026GraphRAGEnabled]. Sarvari and colleagues' generation-assisted vector search (GAVS) framework achieved a statistically significant improvement in fine-grained diagnostic coding recall over a direct LLM baseline (20.63% vs. 17.95%; P < .001), with 98.4% of 63 management plan outputs satisfying all quality criteria [@Sarvari2025Challenges]. The Ollama-driven federated learning approach described by Lal extends these gains to privacy-preserving multi-institutional settings, where patient data remain on-site while LLM agents interface across distributed clinical databases [@Lal2026Ollamadriven].

## Real-World Implementations

The translation from benchmarks to clinical operations is advancing across several distinct implementation classes. TrialMatchAI, developed by Abdallah and colleagues, orchestrates multiple LLM agents across the full pipeline from eligibility extraction to patient-trial recommendation, automating a process historically dependent on manual criteria review and exemplifying the end-to-end research vision — search, analyze, synthesize, act — within a single coordinated system [@Abdallah2026TrialMatchAI]. The ambient AI framework described by Afshar and colleagues reached a weighted median utilization of 65.4% (IQR 50.6–84.0%) across eligible clinical notes in 66 providers spanning eight specialties [@Afshar2025A]. A workflow disruption caused ICD-10 documentation accuracy to plunge from 79% to 35% before recovery, while the internally developed LLM coder maintained a Pearson's r of 0.97 with professional coders — illustrating simultaneously the operational value and the fragility of autonomous documentation agents.

Patient-facing agentic systems have demonstrated compelling clinical trial evidence. In the PreA randomized controlled trial by Tao and colleagues, involving 2,069 patients across 111 specialists and 24 disciplines, an LLM chatbot performing autonomous history-taking and preliminary diagnosis reduced physician consultation duration by 28.7% (3.14 ± 2.25 min vs. 4.41 ± 2.77 min; P < 0.001), improved physician-perceived care coordination by 113.1% (P < 0.001), and improved patient-reported communication ease by 16.0% (P < 0.001) [@Tao2026An]. Equivalent outcomes between autonomous and staff-supported arms confirmed the system's independent operation capability. The community-codesigned LLM chatbot for primary care evaluated by Li and colleagues in Nature Health reinforces that agentic autonomy is not solely a technical property but a social negotiation: the degree of autonomous operation must be calibrated to community trust, local context, and co-design [@Li2026A].

## Challenges and Limitations of Agentic Systems

The evidence converges on a set of persistent challenges that currently constrain safe agentic deployment. Error propagation is the most structurally significant: in a multi-step pipeline, a miscoded clinical variable or hallucinated literature finding propagates through all subsequent steps, producing qualitatively different failure modes than single-step LLM errors. The tenfold token overhead and twofold latency penalty of current agent systems [@Liu2026Benchmarking], combined with the 40–50% safety assessment accuracy documented by Gong and colleagues [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice], signal that agents do not yet reliably execute the cognitively demanding steps that define genuine clinical research competence.

Human oversight requirements remain non-negotiable. The ambient AI episode reported by Afshar and colleagues — where routine workflow changes degraded documentation accuracy by 44 percentage points before human intervention restored baseline — provides a concrete case that even well-validated autonomous systems require continuous monitoring infrastructure [@Afshar2025A]. Zero-shot clinical coding performance reported by Naliyatthaliyazchayil and colleagues compounds this concern: while LLaMA-3.1 achieved 85% primary diagnosis accuracy, ICD-9 code prediction fell to 42.6% and readmission risk prediction to 41.3%, reinforcing the conclusion that autonomous deployment is not currently justifiable without human-in-the-loop oversight [@Naliyatthaliyazchayil2025Evaluating]. Validation frameworks must address failure modes at component interfaces — between retrieval and generation, code execution and interpretation, planning and verification — that standard single-turn benchmarks do not capture. The REFINE reporting checklist provides minimum standards for transparent characterization of model architecture and failure modes in medical AI studies [@Mese2026Reporting], and its extension to multi-component agentic systems will be a prerequisite for building the regulatory evidence base required before agentic coding interfaces in clinical research can be responsibly and broadly deployed.
